summary: Policy-backed control for Search Highlights. Good for quieter and more static search UX, but it removes dynamic content from search home and taskbar or Start search surfaces.

setting.name: Search Highlights
setting.casual_explanation: Search Highlights are the dynamic bits Windows shows in the search area, such as seasonal or suggested content. This policy decides whether those highlights are allowed.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable it when they want a cleaner search experience without dynamic content or suggestions. Others keep it on because they like the extra search home content.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it off makes search more static and less promotional, but it also removes some of the dynamic info Windows tries to surface in search.
setting.professional_notes: This setting maps to EnableDynamicContentInWSB under the Windows Search policy key. Search.admx and the Search Policy CSP both use 0 for disabled and 1 for enabled.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Search Highlights stay enabled by default because the policy is not forcing them off.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes EnableDynamicContentInWSB = 0, which matches the documented disable behavior.

windows_defaults.label: Windows policy default

states.rationale: Policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: Microsoft documents the functional default as enabled. This record still keeps the registry baseline as missing for restore-default semantics.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: People who want the standard Windows search experience
avoid_for: Users who want a quieter search UI
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the default dynamic search experience.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want search to stop surfacing highlights and dynamic content.
tradeoffs: Search can continue showing highlight content.
No forced policy lock-down is applied.

recommended_profiles.label: Minimal search UI
intended_for: Users who want a cleaner search experience
avoid_for: People who like dynamic search content
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want search to look quieter and more static.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the extra info and dynamic content Windows surfaces in search.
tradeoffs: The search surface becomes more minimal.

evidence.title: Microsoft Search Policy CSP
evidence.summary: Official documentation for AllowSearchHighlights and the EnableDynamicContentInWSB registry mapping.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft Search.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file shows AllowSearchHighlights with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0 for EnableDynamicContentInWSB.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes EnableDynamicContentInWSB = 0 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak provenance record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

decision.why: Local Microsoft Search policy files document the exact machine-policy surface and the Search Highlights behavior. The app writes the documented disabled value.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="AllowSearchHighlights" class="Machine" ... key="SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Windows Search" valueName="EnableDynamicContentInWSB">; Search.adml: Disabling this setting turns off search highlights in the start menu search box and in search home.
validation_proof.notes: Local official ADMX defines the exact machine-policy key and value name; local ADML confirms that disabling the policy turns off Search Highlights in Start search and search home.
